result:
# The Weight of Unfinished Books

There's a stack of books on my nightstand that I've been meaning to finish for three years now. They sit there, patient and accusatory, their bookmarks frozen at page 47, page 123, page 89. I used to feel guilty about this. Now I've started to see them differently—as evidence of who I was at different moments, snapshots of curiosities that flared and faded.

We don't talk enough about the books we abandon. There's shame in it, as if putting down a novel halfway through represents some moral failing. But I've come to believe that an unfinished book isn't a failure—it's a conversation that simply ran its course. Sometimes you meet someone at a party and you chat for ten minutes and that's exactly right. You don't need to become lifelong friends with everyone whose company you briefly enjoyed.

I think about the economics of attention more than I probably should. We're living through the greatest abundance of content in human history, and yet we still operate with the scarcity mindset of an earlier era. Finish your plate. Don't waste. See it through. These rules made sense when books were rare, when your village might have three of them and you'd be lucky to hold one in your lifetime. But now? Now we're drowning in words, and the only truly scarce resource is the time we have to take them in.

---

Last week I walked through a neighborhood I hadn't visited since before the pandemic. A coffee shop I used to love was gone—replaced by a smoothie place with too much signage. The bookstore had survived, though, and I spent an hour there, picking things up and putting them down, reading first paragraphs like I was speed-dating ideas.

There's something specific about the smell of a bookstore that triggers a particular kind of hope. It's the hope of possibility, of becoming someone slightly different than who you are right now. Every spine on those shelves represents a door. Most of them you'll never open, and that's fine. The magic is in knowing they're there.

I bought a book about the history of color. Not because I needed to know about the history of color, but because the cover was beautiful and the first page made me curious about cochineal insects and their role in red dyes. Will I finish it? Probably not. But I'll carry it around for a while, and some of its ideas will drift into me through osmosis, and maybe one day at a dinner party I'll mention something about pigments and trade routes, and whoever I'm talking to will think I'm more interesting than I actually am.

---

The older I get, the more I appreciate people who are genuinely enthusiastic about things. Not performed enthusiasm—the kind you see on social media, all exclamation points and "obsessed with this"—but the real thing. The quiet, sustained fascination that makes someone spend twenty years learning about medieval farming techniques or Victorian postal systems or the migratory patterns of arctic terns.

I met a man once who collected antique doorknobs. He could talk for hours about the evolution of lock mechanisms, about the shift from iron to brass, about the specific weight and feel of a doorknob from 1840 versus one from 1890. Most people would find this boring. I found it beautiful. Here was someone who had chosen his territory and committed to knowing it completely. In a world that rewards being superficially interested in everything, he had gone deep instead of wide.

There's courage in that, I think. In saying: this is my thing, and I don't care if you think it's strange. We spend so much energy curating ourselves for public consumption, choosing interests that will make us seem cultured or intelligent or appropriately quirky. The doorknob collector had no such pretensions. He just loved doorknobs.

---

I've been trying to write more by hand lately. There's research suggesting it helps with memory and comprehension, but mostly I do it because it forces me to slow down. When you're typing, you can almost keep pace with your thoughts. When you're writing by hand, your thoughts have to wait for you, and in that waiting, they sometimes transform into something better.

My handwriting has deteriorated terribly since I left school. It was never good to begin with—teachers always commented on it, gently at first, then with increasingly obvious frustration. Now it's almost illegible, even to me. I look back at notes from a few months ago and I might as well be reading ancient Sumerian. But there's something freeing in that illegibility. No one will ever read these pages. They exist purely as a process, a way of thinking out loud without the pressure of an audience.

---

The thing about writing freely is that you quickly discover what's actually on your mind. I sat down planning to write about something clever—maybe the philosophy of personal identity or the strange economics of streaming services—but instead I keep circling back to time. How we spend it. How it runs through our fingers. How we're always standing at the intersection of who we were and who we might become.

My grandmother used to say that the days are long but the years are short. I didn't understand this when I was young. Now I understand it completely. A Tuesday can feel endless. A decade vanishes like morning fog.

So maybe that's what the unfinished books really represent. Not failure, but ambition. The belief, however misguided, that there will be time. That we can always come back to things. That the bookmark is a promise, not a goodbye.

I look at that stack on my nightstand and I see optimism. I see a version of myself who thought she'd have quiet Sunday afternoons stretching out forever. That version wasn't wrong, exactly. She was just measuring in the wrong units.

The books will wait. They're patient. And maybe someday I'll pick one up again, blow off the dust, and remember exactly why I started it in the first place. Or maybe I won't. Either way, they served their purpose. They were there when I needed the possibility of them, and sometimes that's enough.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
